agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation feature for an order management system. Three stakeholders (Product, Finance, Operations) have provided requirements that govern when an order may be cancelled relative to its lifecycle states (placed, invoiced, shipped).","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's request to purchase goods, progressing through lifecycle states from placement to shipment."},{"term":"Cancellation","definition":"The act of voiding an order, preventing further fulfillment. Intended to be available before shipment per product requirements."},{"term":"Invoice","definition":"A financial document that locks in pricing for an order. Described by Finance as irreversible once created."},{"term":"Shipment","definition":"The physical dispatch of goods to the customer. Represents a point after which cancellation is universally agreed to be impossible."},{"term":"Order Placement","definition":"The initial creation/confirmation of an order by a customer. Operations states invoicing occurs at this moment."}]},"business_rules":[],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Customer places an order and immediately requests cancellation","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — Under PM rules, this should succeed (order has not shipped). Under Finance + Ops rules, this must fail (order was invoiced at placement and invoicing is irreversible)."},{"scenario":"Order is placed but shipment is delayed for days","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — PM expects a wide cancellation window (anytime before ship). Finance/Ops rules make cancellation impossible from the moment of placement."},{"scenario":"Partial shipment scenario","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — If part of an order ships, PM rule is ambiguous. Finance rule already blocks cancellation entirely regardless."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"HIGH — If we follow PM rules and allow cancellation of invoiced orders, Finance loses pricing integrity and revenue recognition accuracy. If we follow Finance/Ops rules, customers effectively can never cancel, which increases chargebacks, support costs, and customer churn.","operational":"The Operations team's decision to invoice at placement (to lock pricing) directly creates the conflict. Resolving this may require changing when invoicing occurs, or introducing a concept like 'void' or 'credit note' that reverses an invoice without technically 'cancelling' it.","user":"Customers expect to cancel orders before shipment. If the system silently prevents all cancellations, customer satisfaction and trust will degrade significantly.","risk":"Shipping this feature with the wrong interpretation could violate accounting regulations (if invoices are reversed improperly) or create massive customer experience failures (if cancellation is effectively impossible).","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Cannot cancel orders at all if Finance/Ops rules dominate — severe negative experience."},{"group":"Finance Team","impact":"Revenue recognition, tax reporting, and audit trail integrity are at risk if invoiced orders can be cancelled without a proper reversal mechanism."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"Pricing lock-in strategy is challenged if invoicing must be deferred to preserve a cancellation window."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will bear the burden of angry customers if cancellation is blocked, or will need new workflows for invoice reversal/credit notes."},{"group":"Product Management","impact":"Core feature requirement is undeliverable as stated without resolving the contradiction."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"Three authoritative stakeholders have issued requirements that form a logical contradiction. The PM says cancellation is allowed before shipment. Finance says invoiced orders cannot be cancelled. Operations says orders are invoiced at placement. Taken together: orders are invoiced the instant they are placed, invoiced orders cannot be cancelled, therefore orders can never be cancelled — directly contradicting the PM requirement. No amount of analysis can resolve this without a stakeholder decision. Confidence is below 50 because the spec is internally inconsistent.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction is real and unambiguous — it is not an interpretation issue","The three rules, if all enforced simultaneously, make cancellation impossible","Invoice irreversibility is likely a hard accounting/legal constraint"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether Finance would accept a 'credit note' or 'void' mechanism as distinct from 'cancellation'","Whether Operations could defer invoicing to a later lifecycle stage (e.g., shipment)","Whether the PM requirement was written with awareness of the invoicing policy"],"assumptions":["All three stakeholder statements are current and authoritative policy","'Invoicing' as used by Finance and Operations refers to the same concept","There is no existing credit note or invoice reversal mechanism in the system"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The three requirements form a logical impossibility. (1) PM: orders can be cancelled before shipment. (2) Finance: invoiced orders cannot be cancelled. (3) Operations: orders are invoiced at placement. Therefore, orders become uncancellable at the moment they are placed, leaving zero cancellation window — directly contradicting the PM requirement.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance Team","Operations Team","Customers","Customer Support"],"options":["A: Defer invoicing until shipment, preserving a cancellation window between placement and shipment (satisfies PM, changes Ops process, Finance constraint intact)","B: Invoice at placement but introduce a 'credit note' or 'invoice void' mechanism that is financially distinct from 'cancellation' (satisfies PM intent, preserves Finance language, requires new Finance process)","C: Accept that cancellation is not possible post-placement; PM requirement is updated to only allow cancellation of draft/unpaid orders before placement is confirmed (satisfies Finance and Ops, restricts PM feature)","D: Introduce a time-limited grace period after placement where the invoice is provisional and can be voided (hybrid approach, requires Finance and Ops agreement on grace period duration)"],"recommendation":"Option B is most likely to satisfy all parties — customers get a cancellation-like experience, Finance maintains invoice irreversibility by using credit notes as the reversal mechanism, and Operations keeps invoicing at placement for pricing lock-in. However, this requires Finance to formally agree that credit notes are acceptable. CANNOT proceed without stakeholder alignment."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Requirements as stated are internally contradictory and cannot be implemented faithfully","business_rule_broken":"PM cancellation window is nullified by the combination of Finance invoice irreversibility and Operations invoice-at-placement policy"}]}
